State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance
(916) 322-5662

Administration
322-5660

Executive/Legal
322-5901

Enforcement
322-5441

Statements of Economic Interest
322-6444

March 4, 1981


Michael J. Kersten
Chairperson
Fair Oaks Community Planning
Advisory Council
8224 Hegseth Ct.
Fair Oaks, CA 95628

Dear Mr. Kersten:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice
concerning the disqualification provisions of the Political
Reform Act of 1974.  The following discussion is intended to
give the Council some general guidelines on disqualification.
The facts surrounding each individual decision will have to
be examined in the context of these guidelines on a case-by-
case basis in order to determine whether or not disqualification
is necessary.

     With respect to disqualification, the Political Reform
Act provides that:

          No public official at any level of state or local
     government shall make, participate in making or in any
     way attempt to use his official position to influence a
     governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
     to know he has a financial interest.

                    Government Code Section 87100.1/

     Section 87100 by its terms applies to any official at any
level of state or local government.  Thus a councilmember
must not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt
to use his or her official position to influence a decision
of the Council in which the member has a financial interest.
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700.

_________________________________

1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code.

Michael J. Kerste
March 4, 1981
Page Two


     Section 87103(b) provides that an official has a financial
interest with respect to real property "if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on: .... (b) Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000)." By regulation the Commission
has further defined "material financial effect" in reference
to real property as follows:

     (b) In determining whether it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the effects of a governmental decision
will be significant within the meaning of the general
standard set forth in paragraph (a), consideration
should be given to the following factors:

     . . .

     (2) Whether, in the case of a direct or
indirect interest in real property of one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more held by a public official,
the effect of the decision will be to increase or
decrease:

          (A) The income producing potential of
the property by the lesser of:

               1. One thousand dollars ($1,000)
per month; or

               2. Five percent per month if it is
fifty dollars ($50) or more per month;
or

          (B) The fair market value of the property
by the lesser of:

               1. Ten thousand dollars ($10,000);
or

               2. One half of one percent if it
is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more.

                    2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(2).

     The materiality of a financial effect must be analyzed
on a case-by-case basis. In the situation you posed as an

Michael J. Kersten
March 4, 1981
Page Three


example, the councilmember owns a residence within 500 feet
of a proposed development. The planning application for
that development is before the Council for discussion and a
vote. Without more information, I could not conclude whether
or not the financial effect of a decision by the council on
the member's residence would be material. It would depend
on whether the decision's effect would foreseeably be to
increase or decrease either the income-producing potential
or the fair market value of the property by the thresholds
amounts set forth above in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(2).

You also raised a series of questions on the extent to
which a councilmember who is disqualified from voting may
participate in the proceedings before the Council. When an
official is disqualified he or she must refrain not only
from actually making the decision but also from participating
in the making of the decision or using his or her official
position to influence the decision. Section 87100.

Chairing a meeting during Council deliberations or
hearings on the matter constitutes participation in the
making of a decision and thus would be prohibited. Opinion
requested by Vincent F. Biondo, Jr., 1 FPPC Opinions 54, 58
(No. 75-036, July 2, 1975). Participating as a councilmember
in the deliberations short of voting constitutes an attempt
to use one's official position to influence a governmental
decision and thus would also be prohibited. 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18700(e)(1).

However, the regulations specifically exclude from the
definitions of participating in the making of a governmental
decision and attempting to influence an agency decision the
situation in which the official appears to "represent himself
or herself on matters related solely to his or her personal
interest." 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18700(e)(1), 18700(f)(1).
The "personal interest" of an official has been interpreted
to mean the official's personal financial interests. Accordingly,
a disqualified councilmember may address the Council from
the audience as a member of the general public on a matter
if his or her participation relates solely to his own
financial interest, i.e., his or her residence or business.
This provision does not permit the official to represent
interests other than those which are solely personal. Therefore,
a councilmember may not represent clients or even a group of
his or her neighbors before the Council. A councilmember
may not expressly or impliedly coerce the other councilmembers

Michael J. Kersten
March 4, 1981
Page Four


during a public appearance when he or she appears solely to
represent his or her own personal financial interest. In
addition, a councilmember who appears as a member of the
public in this manner must be subject to the same limitations
as any other member of the public would be in appearing
before the Council.

I trust this letter has been helpful in answering your
concerns. Please feel free to contact me if you would like
to discuss this letter or if you have further questions.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:plh

January 22, 1981

Fair Political Practices Commission
Thomas Houston, Chairman
1100 K Street
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Cal. 95804

Dear Mr. Houston:

Enclosed is a copy of an opinion recently prepared by the County
Counsel of Sacramento County for the Fair Oaks Community Planning
Advisory Council regarding the application of the Political Reform
Act of 1974 to council proceedings. On behalf of the council, I
request that the commission or its legal staff review the opinion
and inform me as to whether you agree with the conclusions reached.

The council is particularly interested in whether Section
18700(d)(2) of Title 2 of the California Administrative Code permits
a council member to participate in the debate as a member of the
public regarding a planning application for a project located
adjacent to the council member's residence (see pages 5 and 6 of
the opinion). How broad is the exclusion made by Section 18700(d)(2)
and what constitutes "personal interests" for purposes of the regulation?

A copy of the letter requesting the county counsel opinion is
also enclosed for your information. Thank you for your assistance
in this matter.

Sincerely,

[illegible]
Michael J. Kersten
Chairperson,
Fair Oaks Community Planning
Advisory Council

cc: Toby Johnson, Sacramento County Board of Supervisors
All council members

Phone:
966-3695 (Home)
445-8545 (Work)

COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO
THE COUNTY COUNSEL
700 H STREET, SUITE 2650
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
PHONE (916) 440-5424

DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSELS

ALFRED J. GUETLING
CLEMENT J. DOUGHERTY, JR.
RYAN M. FOLSTRA
MONTE FULLER
RICHARD D. MAYER
WILLIAM H. FOWLER
JOSEPH N. KLUEPFEL
J. STEVEN BUHRIS
LAWRENCE A. JOHNS
ELAINE F. DIFIETO
MELVYN W. PRICE
MANUEL E. LOPES
LILLY C. FRAWLEY
FRANK M. GARCIA
MARGARET L. HAGERTY
H. DESMOND MCINTOSH
JOEL K. UHER

L. B. ELAM
County Counsel

FRED G. WILLIAMS
Assistant County Counsel

THOMAS A. DARLING
ROBERT S. WILLETT
ROBERT L. PLEINES
Supervising Deputy
County Counsels

December 4, 1980

Michael J. Kersten, Chairperson
Fair Oaks Community Planning Advisory Council
8224 Hegseth Court
Fair Oaks, CA 95628

RE: Conflict of Interest - Political Reform Act 1974
Government Code Section 87100

Dear Mr. Kersten:

We have your letter dated October 1, 1980, which requests we
issue written opinions on the following questions:

1. Must a community council member disqualify himself
or herself when reviewing a planning application involving
property located in the same neighborhood as the residence
of a council member. You indicate that normally this
involves property located within 500 feet of the member's
residence.

2. Must a council member remain silent during
deliberations of the council, if the council member has
a financial interest in a planning application which is
being reviewed by the council?

3. If the council member who has a financial
interest in the planning application is the chairperson
of the council, must that person relinquish the chair
during the council's deliberation on the planning appli-
cation?

4. If a council member has a financial interest in
a planning application, may that council member disclose
the financial interest, disqualify himself from participa-
tion in the deliberation as a council person, but participate

Michael J. Kersten                 -2-                 December 4, 1980


     in the debate and address the council as a member of
     the public? Further, must that council person resign his
     or her membership in the council to participate in the
     debate as a member of the public?

     We are not informed as to the nature of the financial
interest by the letter of October 1, so for the purposes of this
opinion, we assume that the financial interest is a material
financial interest as set forth in County regulations which are
attached as Exhibit "A" to this opinion letter.¹

                              OPINION

     In response to question No. 1, it is our opinion that a coun-
cil member should disqualify himself or herself when his or her
position is so biased so as to substantially color his or her
opinion with regard to the application pending before the coun-
cil.  The proponent for the application is entitled to a fair and
impartial hearing which may not be possible if a council person
has a bias with regard to the pending application.

     In response to questions No. 2 and 3, it is our opinion that
a council person who has a material financial interest in a
matter pending before the council should disclose the financial
interest, disqualify himself or herself from voting on the matter
and refrain from participating in the deliberations of the coun-
cil.  If this involves the chairperson or vice-chairperson, the
chair should be assumed by another council person pursuant to
council rules.

     In response to question No. 4, it is our opinion that the
council person who has disclosed a material financial interest
should not participate in any debate on the matter pending before
the council, except perhaps, in the limited circumstance when he
or she is the owner of, or has a substantial financial interest in
the property which is the subject of the debate.

                              ANALYSIS

     The questions asked involve two completely different concepts
of law.  The first relates to the motive or bias of a trier of
fact and the constitutional demand that a person is entitled to
a fair and impartial hearing.  The other questions deal with
the broad field commonly referred to a "conflict of interest"

_____________
     ¹The County's regulations attached as Exhibit "A" defining
material interests are based upon Government Code Section 87103
and sets forth the nature of the financial interest described in
the Political Reform Act of 1974.

Michael J. Kersten                    -3-                    December 4, 1980

and generally involve an opportunity for personal or pecuniary
gain or aggrandizement.

Your first question deals with whether a proponent will be
afforded a fair hearing. Although a claim of bias or improper
motive can be claimed, to successfully prove a due process viola-
tion due to bias or improper motivation, there must be a suf-
ficient showing that a property owner's rights were jeopardized
by the bias. Case law on the subject has evolved from hearing
decision makers rather than advisory groups but we believe the
rationale of the cases is pertinent particularly when there is
substantial reliance on advisory opinions as in the case of the
opinions given to decision makers by your council. In Sladovich
vs. County of Fresno (1958) 158 Cal. App. 2d 230, 241, the plain-
tiff claimed bias on the part of a planning commissioner because
the commissioner erroneously believed that a particular zoning
classification on plaintiff's property would detrimentally affect
the commissioner's property and that he failed to disclose
interest to the other commissioners. The Court held, however,
that even if such bias existed, it would not necessarily void the
action to remaining members, since all other members voted to
deny the petition and there was no sufficient showing that
plaintiff's rights were jeopardized. It is a general rule that
inquiry into motive appears to be equally applicable to admini-
strative as well as legislative decisions relating to zoning
matters. It has been stated by a court and ruling on a legisla-
tive determination that the "purpose or motive of the city offi-
cials in passing an ordinance is irrelevant to any inquiry
concerning the reasonableness of the ordinance . . . if the con-
ditions justify the enactment of the ordinance, the motives
prompting its enactment are of no consequence. If the conditions
do not justify the enactment, the inquiry as to motive becomes
useless . . ." McCarthy vs. City of Manhattan Beach (1953) 41
Cal. 2d 879, 894. See also Ensign Bickford Realty Corp. vs. City
Council (1977) 68 Cal. App. 3d 467, 478 and Schroeder vs.
Municipal Court (1977) 73 Cal. App. 3d 841, 847.

The general rule also applies to quasi-judicial zoning
determinations. In City of Fairfield vs. Superior Court (1975)
14 Cal. 3d 768, the Supreme Court upheld a city council's admini-
strative denial of an application for a planned unit develop-
ment to permit construction of a shopping center. In so doing,
the Supreme Court ruled that inquiry into the reasoning process
of a council person relative to their rejection of the permit was
improper. Second, the Court held that inquiries seeking to
discover evidence to support a charge that the council person was
biased was also irrelevant.

Each council person who has property proximate to a proposed
development must judge whether he or she is biased with regard to
the project. The question of any bias, of course, can be (and

Michael J. Kersten                    -4-                    December 4, 1980


has in the past) been raised by developers at Planning Commission
and/or Board of Supervisors level when the project passes through
the approval chain.  It would be our recommendatin that if any
question of bias exists, the council person should disqualify
himself and announce the reason therefor.  An exception to this
perhaps would be the situation where the vote of a council person
is necessary in order to take a position by the council on the
particular application.

     Questions 2, 3, and 4 relate to the doctrine of "conflict of
interest".  Under California law, there are four types of
conflicts which can disqualify an individual.  These are generally
as follows:

          1.   A person may not hold two public offices at the
same time, if those offices are incompatible;

          2.   The Political Reform Act of 1974 (Government
Code Section 87100 et seq.) prohibits public officials from
participating in decisions in which they possess an economic
interest;

          3.   Government Code, Section 1090 et seq., prohibits
public officers or employees from participating in
public decisions involving contracts in which they are financially
interested; and

          4.   Government Code Section 1126(a) prohibits
public officers and employees from engaging in incompatible
employment or activities for compensation.

     We believe your activities are controlled by the Political
Reform Act2 (NO. 2 above) and we limit our analysis accordingly.

     The Act precludes certain public officials from participating
in decision making when the official has a financial interest in
the pending matter.  Section 87100 of the Government Code (part
of the Act) reads:

          "No public official at any level of state or
     or local government shall make, participate in
     making or in any way attempt to use his official
     position to influence a governmental decision in
     which he knows or has reason to know he has a
     financial interest."


_______________
2
     Hereinafter "the Act".

Michael J. Kersten                    -5-                    December 4, 1980


     Section 87200 defines the applicability of the Act.

          "This article is applicable to elected
     officers, judges of courts of record, members
     of the Public Utilities Commission, members of
     the State Energy Resources Conservation and
     Development Commission, members of the Fair
     Political Practices Commission, members of the
     board of supervisors, district attorneys and
     chief administrative officers of counties,
     mayors, city managers, chief administrative
     officers and members of city councils of cities,
     members of each of the six regional coastal
     commissions, and to candidates for any of these
     offices at any election."

     It is clear from the above that advisory councils are not
included within the scope of the Act per se. However, the County
of Sacramento has gone beyond the scope of the Act and has speci-
fically included planning advisory councils within the County's
implementation of the Act. Section 2.36.080 of the Sacramento
County Code requires appointees to advisory councils to comply
with all State and County laws, ordinances and regulations
relating to conflicts of interest and financial disclosures. The
County's regulations (Chapter 2.65 of the County Code and regula-
tions adopted thereunder), implementing the Act coupled with the
requirements of Section 2.36.080 make it quite clear that the
Board of Supervisors intended to include all municipal advisory
councils within the orbit of interest regulations. In view of
the reliance of the Board of Supervisors and Planning
Commissioners on council advice, we think this inclusive is
justified. As stated, supra, if a council member has a material
financial interest in an application he or she must not par-
ticipate in the council's deliberations. To do so would conflict
with the County's implementation of the Act. We further believe
the council person must step down from the chair if the conflict
exists.

     Your letter further inquires as to whether the person with
the conflict could participate in the debate before the council
as a member of the public. The Attorney General recently
answered a similar question in regard to a disqualified LAFCO
member who desired to participate as a citizen in a debate or a
proposed annexation to a city. The Attorney General found the
LAFCO member to be disqualified but excepted the member from the
disqualification if the member owned property within the terri-
tory proposed to be annexed. The Attorney General based his opi-
nion on regulations promulgated by the Fair Political Practices
Commission (FPPC) implementing the Act (See 610 Ops. Atty. Gen.
396).

Michael J. Kersten                    -6-                    December 4, 1980


     Based upon the Attorney General's position and the regula-
tions of the FPPC, it is our opinion that a member of your council
who has disqualified himself and announced the basis thereof
may participate in the debate before the council if he has a
substantial interest in or is the owner of property which is the
subject of the council's deliberations. Except in this limited
situation, we are of the opinion the council person should not
participate in the debate.

     We trust this answers the questions you have posed, if there
are additional questions which relate to this opinion, please
feel free to contact the undersigned.

                                        Respectfully submitted,

                                        L. B. ELAM,
                                        County Counsel



                                        By    [illegible]
                                        R. S. WILLETT
                                        Supervising Deputy

RSW:ph

cc:  Tobias Johnson,
     Supervisor, Fifth District
     All Council Members, Fair Oaks
     Community Planning Advisory Council

October 1, 1980


Lee Elam
Sacramento County Counsel
700 H Street
Sacramento, Calif. 95814

Dear Mr. Elam:

     On behalf of the Fair Oaks Community Planning Advisory Council,
I would like to request your written legal opinion on the extent to
which a council member must disqualify himself when reviewing a
planning application for property located in the same neighborhood
as the council member's residence. The most typical fact situation
involves a proposed development of property located within 500 feet
of the member's residence.

     Also, assuming the member has a financial interest in the
decision of whether to approve a particular application, must the
member remain silent during all council deliberations on the
application? In such case, may the member disclose his financial
interest, join the public audience, and address the council regarding
the application solely in his capacity as a neighborhood resident
and private citizen, or must the member resign in order to make
any statement regarding the application? If the financially interested
member is the council chairperson, must he relinquish the chair
for the duration of the deliberations on the application?

     In order to facilitate council action on pending planning
applications, the council would appreciate your response by
November 1, 1980. Thank you.


                                        Sincerely,


                                        Michael J. Kersten
                                        Chairperson,
                                        Fair Oaks Community Planning
                                        Advisory Council

cc: Toby Johnson, Sacramento County Board of Supervisors
    All council members

nion on regulations promUlgated by the Fair Political Practices

Michael J. Kersten

Based upon the Attorney General's position and the regula-
tions of the FPPC, it is our opinion that a member of your council

sections.conclusion: A council member should disqualify himself or herself when his or her position is so biased so as to substantially color his or her opinion with regard to the application pending before the council. A council person who has a material financial interest in a matter pending before the council should disclose the financial interest, disqualify himself or herself from voting on the matter and refrain from participating in the deliberations of the council. A disqualified council member may appear before the council as a member of the public on matters related solely to his or her personal financial interest, but must refrain from participating in deliberations as a council member.

sections.question_synthetic: The Fair Oaks Community Planning Advisory Council seeks guidance on conflict of interest and disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act of 1974, specifically whether council members must disqualify themselves from planning applications affecting nearby properties, whether they must remain silent during deliberations if financially interested, and whether they may participate as members of the public if they disclose their interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Council members with material financial interests must disqualify themselves from voting and deliberating on the matter. However, a disqualified member may participate as a member of the public to address matters related solely to their personal financial interest, such as their residence or business, without resigning from the council. The scope of participation is limited to appearing and representing personal interests in the same manner as any other member of the public.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document is OCR-scanned with significant degradation and scanning artifacts affecting readability throughout. Contains multiple embedded documents: initial request from council chairperson (January 22, 1981), county counsel opinion (December 4, 1980), and FPPC response letter (March 4, 1981). FPPC response by Diane Maura Fishburn clarifies and elaborates on county counsel's opinion regarding Government Code Sections 87100, 87103, and administrative code sections 18700, 18702. High OCR error rate reduces confidence in exact verbatim text extraction, but core substantive content is discernible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: of bias exists, the council person should disqualify 
himself and announce the reason therefor. 
An exception to this 
perhaps would be the situation where the vote of a council person 
is necessary in order to take a position by the council on the 
particular application. 
Questions 2, 3, and 4 relate to the doctrine of "conflict of 
interest". 
Under California law; there are four types of 
conflicts which can disqualify an individual. 
These are generally 
as follows: 
1. 
A person may not hold two public offices at the 
same time, if those offices are incompatible; 
2. 
The Political Reform Act of 1974 (Government 
Code Section 87100 et seq.) prohibits public officials from 
participating in decisions in which they possess an economic 
interest; 
3. 
Government Code, Section 1090 et seq., prohibits 
public officers or employees from participating in 
decisions involving contracts in which they are financially 
interested; and 
4. 
Government Code Section 1126(a) prohibits 
public officers and employees from engaging in incompatible 
employment or activities for compensation. 
We believe your activities are controlled by the Political 
Reform Act2 
(No. 2 above) and we limit our analysis accordingly. 
The Act precludes certain public officials from participating 
in decision making when the official has a financial interest in 
the pending matter. 
Section 87100 of the Government Code (part 
of the Act) reads: 
2 
"No public official at any level of state or 
or local government shall make, participate in 
making or in any way attempt to use his official 
position to influence a governmental decision in 
which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest." 
Hereinafter "the Act". 


Michael J. Kersten 
December 4, 1980 
Section 87200 defines the applicability of the Act. 
"This article is applicable to elected 
officers, judges of courts of record, meillbers 
of the Public utilities Commission, members of 
the State Energy Resources Conservation and 
Development Commission, members of the Fair 
Political Practices Commission, members of the 
board of supervisors, district attorneys and 
chief administrative officers of counties, 
mayors, city managers, chief administrative 
officers and members of city councils of cities, 
members of each of the six regional coastal 
commissions, and to candidates for any of these 
offices at any election." 
It is clear from the above that advisory councils are not 
included within the scope of the Act per see 
However, the County 
of Sacramento has gone beyond the scope of the Act and has speci-
fically included Planning Advisory Councils within the County's 
implementation of the Act. 
Section 2.36.080 of the Sacramento 
County Code requires appointees to advisory councils to comply 
with all State and County laws, ordinances and regulations 
relating to conflicts of interest and financial disclosures. 
The 
County's regulations (Chapter 2.65 of the County Code and regula-
tions adopted thereunder), implementing the Act coupled with the 
requirements of Section 2.36.080 make it quite clear that the 
Board of Supervisors intended to include all municipal advisory 
councils within the conflict of interest regulations. 
In view of 
the reliance of the Board of Supervisors and Planning 
Commissioners on council advice, we think this inclusive is 
justified. 
As stated, supra, if a council member has a material 
financial interest in an application he or she must not par-
ticipate in the council's deliberations. 
To do so would conflict 
with the County's implementation of the Act. 
We further believe 
the council person must step down from the chair if the conflict 
exists. 
Your letter further inquires as to whether the person with 
the conflict could participate in the debate before the council 
as a member of the public. 
The Attorney General recently 
answered a similar question in regard to a disqualified LAFCO 
member who desired to participate as a citizen in a debate or a 
proposed annexation to a city. 
The Attorney General found the 
LAFCO member to be disqualified but excepted the member from the 
disqualification if the member owned property within the terri-
tory proposed to be annexed. 
The Attorney General based his opi-
nion on regulations promUlgated by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission (FPPC) implementing the Act (See 610 Ops. Atty. Gen. 
396) . 


Michael J. Kersten 
Dece~ber 4, 1980 
Based upon the Attorney General's position and the regula-
tions of the FPPC, it is our opinion that a member of your council 
who has disqualified himself and announced the basis thereof 
may participate in the debate before the council if he has a 
substantial interest in or is the owner of property which is the 
subject of the council's deliberations. 
Except in this limited 
situation, we are of the opinion the council person should not 
participate in the debate. 
We trust this answers the questions you have posed, if there 
are additional questions which relate to this opinion, please 
feel free to contact the undersigned. 
RSW:ph 
cc: 
Tobias Johnson, 
Supervisor, Fifth District

CONCLUSION: A council member should disqualify himself or herself when his or her position is so biased so as to substantially color his or her opinion with regard to the application pending before the council. A council person who has a material financial interest in a matter pending before the council should disclose the financial interest, disqualify himself or herself from voting on the matter and refrain from participating in the deliberations of the council. A disqualified council member may appear before the council as a member of the public on matters related solely to his or her personal financial interest, but must refrain from participating in deliberations as a council member.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance (916) 322-5662 Administration 322-5660 Executive/Legal 322-5901 Enforcement 322-5441 Statements of Economic Interest 322-6444 March 4, 1981 Michael J. Kersten Chairperson Fair Oaks Community Planning Advisory Council 8224 Hegseth Ct. Fair Oaks, CA 95628 Dear Mr. Kersten: This letter is in response to your request for advice concerning the disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974. The following discussion is intended to give the Council some general guidelines on disqualification. The facts surrounding each individual decision will have to be examined in the context of these guidelines on a case-by- case basis in order to determine whether or not disqualification is necessary. With respect to disqualification, the Political Reform Act provides that: No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Government Code Section 87100.1/ Section 87100 by its terms applies to any official at any level of state or local government. Thus a councilmember must not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or her official position to influence a decision of the Council in which the member has a financial interest. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700. _________________________________ 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code. Michael J. Kerste March 4, 1981 Page Two Section 87103(b) provides that an official has a financial interest with respect to real property "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: .... (b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000)." By regulation the Commission has further defined "material financial effect" in reference to real property as follows: (b) In determining whether it is reasonably fore- seeable that the effects of a governmental decision will be significant within the meaning of the general standard set forth in paragraph (a), consideration should be given to the following factors: . . . (2) Whether, in the case of a direct or indirect interest in real property of one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more held by a public official, the effect of the decision will be to increase or decrease: (A) The income producing potential of the property by the lesser of: 1. One thousand dollars ($1,000) per month; or 2. Five percent per month if it is fifty dollars ($50) or more per month; or (B) The fair market value of the property by the lesser of: 1. Ten thousand dollars ($10,000); or 2. One half of one percent if it is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(2). The materiality of a
embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter addresses conflict of interest requirements for a planning advisory council under the Political Reform Act of 1974, providing guidance on disqualification procedures, disclosure requirements, and the limited circumstances under which a disqualified council member may participate as a member of the public.
